title: Chemical Bonding & Molecular Structure Test - Chapter Test

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Why a fielder in the ground gradually pulls his hands backward while catching a fast moving cricket ball ?
options: To reduce the acceleration of the ball
To increase the acceleration of the ball
To increase the velocity of the ball
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 1: To reduce the acceleration of the ball.

Explanation:

When a fielder catches a fast-moving cricket ball, the ball has a large momentum. To stop it, the fielder has to bring its momentum to zero.

We know:

$$ \text{Force} = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} $$

where:

  • $\Delta p$ = change in momentum

  • $\Delta t$ = time taken

The change in momentum is fixed because the ball has to be stopped. So, if the fielder pulls his hands backward, the time of stopping $\Delta t$ increases.

When time increases, the force decreases.

Also, since acceleration is related to force by

$$ F = ma $$

less force means less acceleration (actually less retardation).

So, by moving his hands backward, the fielder reduces the acceleration of the ball, and therefore the force on his hands becomes less. This prevents injury.

Final Answer:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1: To reduce the acceleration of the ball}} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following graphs represents the equation of motion v = u + at; where all quantities are non-zero and symbols carry their usual meanings?
questions.solution: The slope of displacement time graph gives velocity. If slope changes then velocity changes. In graph $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{c}$ the slope is constant and hence the velocity is constant. Therefore acceleration is zero. In graph (d) the acceleration has non zero value.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Shown in the figure are two hollow cubes C1 and C2 of negligible mass partially filled (depicted by darkened area) with liquids of densities ρ1 and ρ2, respectively, floating in water (density ρw). The relationship between ρ1, ρ2 and ρw is
options: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw
ρ1 < ρw < ρ2
questions.solution:
Key Idea:

When something floats, it means it is lighter (has a lower average density) than the liquid it is in.

For floating, the weight of water pushed aside by the object is equal to the weight of the object.

If an object sinks deeper (is more submerged), it means its average density is higher.

For hollow cubes with very small weight, the heaviness (density) of the cube comes only from the liquid that is inside them.

What the Figure Shows:

The figure has two cubes, C1 and C2, each filled with a different liquid. The densities of these liquids are ρ1 and ρ2.

C2 is deeper in the water than C1. This means C2 is heavier (has a greater density) than C1.

Because the cubes themselves do not weigh much, the only thing that affects how deep they float is the density of the liquid inside.

So, the density ρ1 (inside C1) is greater than ρ2 (inside C2).

Both cubes float. This tells us that the density of the liquids inside must be less than the density of water (ρw), or otherwise they would sink.

Putting all this together:

ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

Answer: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is dimensionless quantity?
questions.solution:
Strain is the only dimensionless quantity among the four.

• Stress has units of force per area:

$$[{\rm Stress}] = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{MLT^{-2}}{L^2} = M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}.$$

• Pressure is the same dimensionally as stress:

$$[{\rm Pressure}] = M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}.$$

• Force has units:

$$[F] = M\,L\,T^{-2}.$$

• Strain is a ratio of lengths:

$$\text{Strain} = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

so all the length dimensions cancel, making it dimensionless.

Answer: Option 2 (Strain).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A railway wagon (open at the top) of mass M<sub>1</sub> is moving with speed v<sub>1</sub> along a straight track. As a result of rain, after some time it gets partially filled with water so that the mass of the wagon becomes M<sub>2</sub> and speed becomes v<sub>2</sub>. Taking the rain to be falling vertically and the water stationery inside the wagon, the relation between the two speeds v<sub>1</sub> and v<sub>2</sub> is :
options: $\frac{1}{2}$M1$v^2_1$ < $\frac{1}{2}$ M2$v^2_2$​
M1v1 = M2v2
M1v1 < M2v2
questions.solution:
Let's analyze the problem. Since the rain is falling vertically and the water inside the wagon is stationary, the horizontal momentum of the system (wagon + water) is conserved.

Conservation of Momentum

The initial horizontal momentum of the system is given by: $$ M_1 v_1 $$

The final horizontal momentum of the system, after the wagon has collected water, is: $$ M_2 v_2 $$

According to the principle of conservation of momentum: $$ M_1 v_1 = M_2 v_2 $$

Therefore, the correct relation between the two speeds $ v_1 $ and $ v_2 $ is given by Option C: $$ M_1 v_1 = M_2 v_2 $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Escape speed from the Earth is close to 11.2 km s⁻¹. On another planet whose radius is half of the Earth's radius and whose mass density is four times that of the Earth, the escape speed in km s⁻¹ will be close to :
questions.solution:
To find the escape speed from another planet given its radius and mass density in relation to Earth's, we need to use the concept of escape velocity. The escape velocity $ v_e $ from a spherical body is given by:

$$ v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}} $$

where:

  • $ G $ is the gravitational constant
  • $ M $ is the mass of the planet
  • $ R $ is the radius of the planet

We know the escape speed from Earth is 11.2 km/s. Therefore:

$$ v_{e, \text{Earth}} = 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

We denote the escape velocity of the new planet as $ v_{e, \text{planet}} $. Let's express the mass of the new planet in terms of Earth's mass.

The mass $ M $ of a planet can be written as the product of its volume and density:

$$ M = \rho V $$

For a spherical body:

$$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 $$

Therefore:

$$ M \propto \rho R^3 $$

Given the new planet's radius $ R_p $ is half of Earth's radius $ R_e $:

$$ R_p = \frac{R_e}{2} $$

Also, its density $ \rho_p $ is four times the density of Earth $ \rho_e $:

$$ \rho_p = 4 \rho_e $$

The new planet's mass $ M_p $ compared to Earth's mass $ M_e $ can be written as:

$$ M_p \propto \rho_p R_p^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e}{2}\right)^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e^3}{8}\right) = \frac{\rho_e R_e^3}{2} = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Thus:

$$ M_p = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Now we substitute $ M_p $ and $ R_p $ into the escape velocity formula:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_p}{R_p}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G \frac{M_e}{2}}{\frac{R_e}{2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_e}{R_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{G M_e}{R_e}} $$

This is because the factor of 2 in the denominator of the mass term and the factor of 2 in the radius term cancels out. So:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} v_{e, \text{Earth}} $$

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

Calculating this value:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = 11.2 \sqrt{2} \approx 11.2 \cdot 1.414 = 15.8 \text{ km/s} $$

Thus, the correct option is:\p>

Option B

15.8

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A sound wave has a frequency of 1 kHz and wavelength 50 cm. How long will it take to travel 1 km?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The AC mains domestic supply current in India changes direction in every
questions.solution:
The domestic AC in India has a frequency of 50 Hz.

One full cycle period:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50}\text{ s} = 0.02\text{ s}$$

It reverses direction every half-cycle:

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{50}\text{ s} = \frac{1}{100}\text{ s} = 0.01\text{ s}$$

Answer: Option 4, $\tfrac{1}{100}$ s.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is an example of Second Class Lever?
questions.solution:
To determine which of these options exemplifies a Second Class Lever, we need to first understand the characteristics of the three types of levers.

A lever is defined by its three components: the effort, the load, and the fulcrum. These are arranged differently in each class:

First Class Lever: The fulcrum is situated between the effort and the load. An example is a seesaw.

Second Class Lever: The load is located between the effort and the fulcrum. An example is a wheelbarrow.

Third Class Lever: The effort is applied between the fulcrum and the load. An example is a pair of tweezers.

Now, let's examine each option:

Option A: A pair of scissors operates as a First Class Lever because the fulcrum is between the effort applied by the hand and the load exerted by the blades on the paper.

Option B: A bottle opener functions as a Second Class Lever. Here, the fulcrum is at one end (where the opener pivots on the bottle cap), the effort is applied at the handle, and the load (the resistance of the bottle cap) sits in the middle.

Option C: A cricket bat is an example of a Third Class Lever. The effort (force of the player's hands) is applied between the fulcrum (where the handle is gripped) and the load (the ball hitting the bat).

Option D: A bow and arrow do not conform to the typical lever classifications as they primarily involve the concepts of tension and elasticity, rather than the mechanical advantage given by levers.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B: A bottle opener

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->An incandescent electric bulb converts 20% of its power consumption into light, and the remaining power is dissipated as heat. The bulb's filament has a resistance of 200 Ω and 2 A current flows through it. If the bulb remains ON for 10 h and the rate of electricity charge is Rs. 5/unit, then which among the following is the correct amount for the money spent on producing light?
questions.solution:
Power input of the bulb

$$P = I^2R = (2\text{ A})^2 \times 200\ \Omega = 4\times200 = 800\text{ W} = 0.8\text{ kW}$$

Total energy consumed in 10 h

$$E_{\rm tot} = P\times t = 0.8\ \text{kW}\times10\ \text{h} = 8\ \text{kWh}$$

Total cost of electricity at Rs. 5 per unit (1 unit=1 kWh)

$$\text{Cost}_{\rm tot} = 8\ \text{kWh}\times5\ \text{Rs./kWh} = 40\ \text{Rs.}$$

Only 20% of that goes into light

$$\text{Cost}_{\rm light} = 0.20\times40\ \text{Rs.} = 8\ \text{Rs.}$$

Answer: Rs. 8 (Option 4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The potential difference between the two end terminals of an electric heater is 220 V and the current through it is 0.5 A. What would be the current through the heater if the potential difference across the terminals of the heater is reduced to 120 V?
questions.solution:
To determine the current through the heater when the potential difference is reduced to 120 V, we can use Ohm's Law, which states:

$$ V = IR $$

where:

  • $$ V $$ is the potential difference (voltage)
  • $$ I $$ is the current
  • $$ R $$ is the resistance

First, we need to find the resistance of the heater using the initial conditions. Given that the initial potential difference $$ V_1 $$ is 220 V, and the initial current $$ I_1 $$ is 0.5 A:

$$ R = \frac{V_1}{I_1} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ R = \frac{220 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ A}} $$ = 440 $$ \Omega $$

Now, we need to find the current $$ I_2 $$ when the potential difference across the heater, $$ V_2 $$, is reduced to 120 V using the same resistance. By applying Ohm's Law again:

$$ I_2 = \frac{V_2}{R} $$

Substituting the new potential difference and the resistance:

$$ I_2 = \frac{120 \text{ V}}{440 \Omega} $$

Calculating the value:

$$ I_2 \approx 0.273 \text{ A} $$

Therefore, the current through the heater would be approximately 0.27 A when the potential difference is 120 V.

The correct answer is:

Option C

0.27 A

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following metals is most commonly used for making filament of incandescent electric bulbs?
questions.solution:
The metal most commonly used for making the filament of incandescent electric bulbs is Option D: Tungsten.

Tungsten is chosen for this purpose due to its extremely high melting point of about 3422°C (6192°F), which allows it to remain solid and emit light at the high temperatures required for incandescence. Additionally, tungsten has a relatively low rate of evaporation at these high temperatures, which extends the life of the filament. Its durability and efficiency in converting electrical energy into light make it the ideal material for this application.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: When light scattered by a molecule and the frequency of the scattered light is changed, this phenomenon is called
questions.solution:
The phenomenon where light is scattered by a molecule and the frequency of the scattered light is changed is known as the Raman effect.

During Raman scattering, when a photon interacts with a molecule, it can either give energy to the molecule, raising its vibrational state (Stokes scattering), or take energy away from it, lowering its vibrational state (anti-Stokes scattering). This results in a frequency shift of the scattered light relative to the incident light.

The Raman effect is a form of inelastic scattering, named after Indian physicist C.V. Raman, who discovered this effect in 1928. It plays a crucial role in chemical analysis and molecular spectroscopy.

Option B: Raman effect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Two identical spring balances S1 and S2 are connected one after the other and are held vertically as shown in the figure. A mass of 10 kg is hanging from S2. If the readings on S1 and S2 are W1 and W2 respectively, then :
questions.solution:
Step 1:

Let the hanging mass be $m = 10 \,\text{kg}$.

The weight of the mass is

$$ W = mg = 10 \times 9.8 = 98 \,\text{N}.$$

For convenience, in such problems, instead of Newton, we express the balance reading directly in “kg‑weight.”

So, the force corresponds to 10 kg‑weight.

Step 2:

Spring balance S₂ is directly attached to the mass.

Therefore, S₂ supports the whole 10 kg mass.

So,

$$ W_2 = 10 \,\text{kg}. $$

Step 3:

Now look at spring balance S₁.

It is supporting both S₂ and the 10 kg mass hanging below it.

Thus, S₁ also bears the same total weight of $10 \,\text{kg}$.

So,

$$ W_1 = 10 \,\text{kg}. $$

Final Answer:

$$ W_1 = 10 \,\text{kg}, \quad W_2 = 10 \,\text{kg} $$

Correct Option: D ✅

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements with regard to the phenomenon of the primary rainbow formation by water droplets is/are correct?

1. It involves refraction and one internal reflection of sunlight.

2. It involves refraction of sunlight only.

3. It is formed as the inner bow.

4. It may involve more than one internal reflection as well as refraction of sunlight.

Select the answer using the code given below :
questions.solution:
In order to understand the correct statements regarding the phenomenon of primary rainbow formation by water droplets, we need to delve into the physics of light behavior. A primary rainbow is a result of the interplay between refraction, reflection, and dispersion of sunlight within water droplets. Let's analyze the statements:

1. It involves refraction and one internal reflection of sunlight.

This statement is correct. When sunlight enters a water droplet, it bends (refracts). Inside the droplet, the light reflects off the inner surface and then refracts again as it exits the droplet. This process creates the spectrum of colors seen in a primary rainbow.

2. It involves refraction of sunlight only.

This statement is incorrect. As explained above, the formation of a primary rainbow involves both refraction and internal reflection, not just refraction alone.

3. It is formed as the inner bow.

This statement is correct. The primary rainbow is the inner bow, and its colors are visible in the sequence of red on the outer edge to violet on the inner edge.

4. It may involve more than one internal reflection as well as refraction of sunlight.

This statement is incorrect with regard to the primary rainbow but applies to secondary rainbows. A secondary rainbow is formed by two internal reflections within the water droplet, leading to a relatively fainter bow outside the primary rainbow with reverse color sequence.

Given this analysis, the correct statements are 1 and 3. Therefore, the answer is:

Option B:

1 and 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Ramesh cannot see distinctly objects kept beyond 2 m. This defect can be corrected by using a lens of power
questions.solution:
Ramesh's condition is known as myopia or nearsightedness, where he can't see objects clearly beyond a certain distance, in this case, 2 meters. To correct myopia, concave lenses (which have negative focal lengths) are typically used, because they help to diverge light rays before they reach the eye, thus moving the focal point back onto the retina.

We need to find the power of the lens required to correct his vision. The formula for the lens power $ P $ in diopters (D) is given by:

$$ P = \frac{1}{f} $$

where $ f $ is the focal length in meters. For Ramesh, the far point is at 2 meters, meaning the lens should have its focal length $ f $ set to -2 meters (negative because it's a concave lens). Substituting the focal length into the formula, we get:

$$ P = \frac{1}{-2 \text{ m}} = -0.5 \text{ D} $$

Therefore, the correct lens power needed to correct Ramesh's vision is -0.5 diopters.

So, the correct option is:

Option B: -0.5 D

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A positive charge is moving towards south in a space where magnetic field is pointing in the north direction. The moving charge will experience :
options: a deflecting force towards north direction.
a deflecting force towards east direction.
a deflecting force towards west direction.
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option D (no deflecting force). Here's why:

Understanding the Concept:

Magnetic Force on a Moving Charge: A charged particle moving in a magnetic field experiences a force only if its velocity has a component perpendicular to the magnetic field direction.

Right-Hand Rule: You can determine the direction of the magnetic force using the right-hand rule. However, in this scenario, we don't need to apply the rule.

Explanation:

Since the positive charge is moving parallel (towards South) to the magnetic field (pointing North), there is no perpendicular component of velocity.

In simpler terms, the charge is moving "along" the magnetic field lines, not "across" them. Therefore, it experiences no magnetic force to deflect it.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: What will happen if a collection of positive and negative charges are passed at a high speed through a magnetic field which is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charges? (Assume that both kind of charges are NOT going to recombine)
options: Both kind of charges will stop moving
Positive charges and negative charges will separate out
Positive charges will stop but negative charges will continue moving uninterrupted
Both kind of charges will keep moving uninterrupted
questions.solution:
When a collection of positive and negative charges is passed at high speed through a magnetic field that is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charges, the charges experience the Lorentz force, which acts perpendicular to both their velocity and the magnetic field.

Lorentz Force

The force experienced by a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by:

$ \mathbf{F} = q (\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}) $

where:

  • $ \mathbf{F} $ is the force on the charge

  • $ q $ is the charge of the particle

  • $ \mathbf{v} $ is the velocity of the particle

  • $ \mathbf{B} $ is the magnetic field

Effects of Lorentz Force on Charges


  1. Positive charges: They will experience a force in a direction given by the right-hand rule, causing them to curve in one direction.

  2. Negative charges: They will experience a force in the opposite direction to the positive charges (due to the negative sign of their charge), causing them to curve in the opposite direction.

Key Points:


  • Both positive and negative charges experience the Lorentz force.

  • The Lorentz force will indeed cause the charges to move in curved paths rather than straight lines.

  • Despite the curved paths, the charges will continue to move unless there is some other external force or condition that stops them.

  • The problem states that both types of charges are not going to recombine, meaning they will continue moving in their respective curved paths due to the Lorentz force.

Conclusion:

Both kinds of charges will keep moving, albeit in curved paths due to the Lorentz force acting on them. This does not mean they will move uninterrupted in straight lines, but they will not stop or separate based on the information given.

Therefore, the correct interpretation aligns with the explanation:

Option D: Both kinds of charges will keep moving uninterrupted.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A glass prism splits white light into different colours. This phenomenon is called dispersion of light by prism. Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Red light will deviate the most and it is because of the reflection of light
Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light
Red light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light
Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the reflection of light
questions.solution:
Option B is correct: Violet light will deviate the most and it is because of the refraction of light.

When white light passes through a glass prism, it is dispersed into its constituent colors due to refraction. Each color of light bends at a different angle because each color has a different wavelength and refractive index. Violet light, having the shortest wavelength, is refracted the most, while red light, having the longest wavelength, is refracted the least. This differential bending of light waves causes the splitting of white light into its spectral colors, known as dispersion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The focal length of a concave lens is 0.5 m. The power of the lens is
questions.solution:
For a lens, power is given by

$$ P = \frac{1}{f} $$

where $f$ is in metre.

Since it is a concave lens, its focal length is negative:

$$ f = -0.5 \, \text{m} $$

Now,

$$ P = \frac{1}{-0.5} = -2 \, \text{D} $$

So, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{-2.0 \, \text{D}} $$

Option 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: One parsec in astronomical units (A.U.) is about
questions.solution:
We know:

  • $1 \, \text{parsec} = 3.26 \, \text{light years}$

  • $1 \, \text{light year} \approx 6.3 \times 10^4 \, \text{A.U.}$

So,

$$ 1 \, \text{parsec} = 3.26 \times 6.3 \times 10^4 \, \text{A.U.} $$

$$ \approx 20.5 \times 10^4 \, \text{A.U.} $$

$$ = 2.05 \times 10^5 \, \text{A.U.} $$

So, one parsec is about:

$$ 2 \times 10^5 \, \text{A.U.} $$

Correct option: Option 3
